html:
Noumi Limited: A Distressed Equity Stub With a Real Consumer Brand Inside

Noumi is not the negative-enterprise-value cash box described in the older draft. That is stale and materially wrong. The current opportunity is more speculative, but still interesting: a tiny ordinary equity stub on a real Australian branded beverage business whose operating performance is improving while the capital structure looks almost impossible.

At roughly $0.07 for NOUMF, tracking ASX:NOU around A$0.105-A$0.115, Noumi’s ordinary equity is valued near US$19 million, or about A$29 million. Against that, H1 FY26 revenue rose 11.2% to A$332.7 million and adjusted operating EBITDA rose 23.3% to A$33.9 million in the latest company presentation. Q3 FY26 then added A$157 million of revenue, up 11.1%, with A$17.7 million of operating cash inflow. This is not a dying food manufacturer.

The reason the stock is abandoned is the May 2027 convertible-note maturity. At December 2025, Noumi had only A$12.5 million of cash, A$80.7 million of financial debt excluding converts, A$95.6 million of lease liabilities, and convertible notes with A$593.6 million redemption value, rising toward A$610.4 million at maturity. The auditor and company explicitly flag material uncertainty around the refinancing. Ordinary equity is junior to a huge secured claim.

That is the whole setup. Noumi’s common stock is not cheap on true EV/EBITDA if the notes are treated as debt. It is cheap as an option on a recapitalization that lets common holders keep some slice of a much better operating company.

The business underneath is now worth caring about. Milklab remains the crown jewel: a barista-focused plant-based and dairy milk franchise with genuine channel credibility. In H1 FY26, overall Milklab sales rose 11.1%, plant-based retail surged 51.8%, Milklab Oat rose 22.5%, and plant export sales grew 18.4%. The plant-based segment still produced A$22.9 million of adjusted EBITDA at a 24.3% margin despite deliberately heavier marketing spend. That is a branded CPG asset, not a commodity dairy line.

The surprise is that Dairy & Nutritionals has stopped being just a drag. H1 FY26 revenue rose 15.7% to A$238.4 million, adjusted EBITDA more than doubled to A$12.4 million, and margins improved from 2.2% to 5.2%. Export long-life dairy rebounded 69.2%, bulk cream sales improved, and consumer nutritionals were positive. If this segment can hold mid-single-digit EBITDA margins while Milklab keeps compounding, FY26 adjusted EBITDA plausibly lands around A$65-A$75 million, versus A$57.4 million in FY25.

The market is not pricing that operating inflection because the stock is screened as a fraud-tainted, overlevered penny stock. Retail attention is thin, not euphoric. HotCopper discussion around the H1 result and future outlook is sparse, and the OTC line is even more obscure. The brand has consumer relevance; the stock has almost no institutional relevance. That mismatch is where the torque sits.

The central question is not whether Noumi can repay A$610 million in cash. It almost certainly cannot. The question is whether noteholders maximize value by zeroing the ordinary equity, or by equitizing/refinancing in a way that preserves the listing, avoids value-destructive distress, and lets the operating business keep growing. A hard wipeout is possible. But a consensual outcome is also rational, especially if FY26 proves the business can support a cleaner post-recap structure.

The conversion terms matter. Tranche A converts at A$0.70 and Tranche B at A$0.32, far above the current share price. Contractual conversion would be massively dilutive but not necessarily fatal to existing holders; a negotiated equitization could be worse or better depending on leverage, control, and whether existing major holders fight for a stub. The common is therefore a restructuring security, not a normal consumer-staples equity.

The upside case is that by the time negotiations crystallize, Noumi is visibly a A$70 million-plus EBITDA business with a 20%-plus-margin Milklab platform and a stabilized dairy segment. A strategic buyer or noteholder-led recap could value the business at 6.5x-8.0x EBITDA, especially if Milklab is separated in investors’ minds from the old Freedom Foods scandal. On A$75 million of EBITDA, that is A$490-A$600 million of enterprise value before giving credit for further Milklab growth. If old equity retains even 10%-20% of the cleaned-up equity, today’s A$29 million market cap can still multiply.

The downside is severe. If noteholders demand economics close to full recovery and leave current common with only a token stub, NOUMF can trade near zero. There is no net-cash floor. The protection is not balance-sheet asset value; it is the operating value of Milklab and the incentive to avoid destroying a functioning company during recapitalization.

The cleanest variant perception is: Noumi is not a cheap balance sheet. It is a cheap claim on negotiation leverage around a real branded beverage business whose earnings are moving in the right direction. That is riskier than the old thesis, but more intellectually honest.

Bear target: $0.01, down 86%. The recap wipes out most common value and existing shareholders keep only a symbolic stub.

Base target: $0.16, up 129%. FY26/FY27 EBITDA reaches A$70-A$75 million, the notes are equitized or refinanced with existing equity retaining roughly 12%-18% of the post-recap value.

Bull target: $0.38, up 443%. Milklab earns a real branded-platform valuation, Dairy & Nutritionals holds its recovery, and a negotiated conversion or strategic transaction leaves current holders with meaningful participation in a A$600 million-plus enterprise.
